speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Eva Weiss, November 16, 1995, tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so Auschwitz was a realization that you don't do nothing. You're aimlessly there, when they're going to come in and when they're going to finish us. What's going to be the end? We don't know. And we don't know about the family. That was-- you know, I don't-- the only thing I ever said with my sister, the only thing we hoped, that my mother didn't witness all this. That she didn't suffer. That's the only thing. We already knew about crematorium. And we knew, probably, that's where they went with my little brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we're in Auschwitz, that's when we got the number. OK? And this is middle of July, and I remember Mengele, the doctor. And we always the main concern-- we didn't concern about nothing. We don't know who was in the next bunk, huge barracks, rows of rows of barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say 30, 40 barracks as far as you can see-- each lager, about, you know, one-- like a street, and across the street and facing you. I don't know the number of the barrack, but I know it was a C lager. And after they got through with us counting, or punishing us and staying-- they said that platz, they had to stay in there-- we always managed the five, you know, get up and everything. And it's my sister in the front, and so me, and then my cousins who were the same height as I am. And everything was working out fine where we were. And one day, I remember wandering away in Auschwitz. I just-- after, when you were finished and you could go-- it was a huge, like a baseball field. And end of the-- end of this street part, and that's where they all gathered. And I cannot-- that picture I'll never forget. Because as far as you could see your eyes-- and here I am wandering as a kid, just faces of women. You don't know who they are. I mean, just unbelievable. And some would talk to you. While some you didn't want to talk to. You just wandered aimlessly and wandered. One day I wandered towards the kitchen of the smell-- which was opposite end. Not where the field was, where everybody gathered. But you couldn't go back to the barrack, by the way. During the day, no. You went only when the food was coming, afternoon, again at night time, I mean. And again the they had to be in platz, and they counted you again for hours. And if anyone was missing, you were punished. You didn't get the food like 5 or 6 o'clock. You got it after. I don't even know. I think once or twice they took us to a community bathroom. I-- I mean a-- a shower place. But when they took us, and we had to get out of our barracks, or our lager, the C lager, you were always ready that this is the crematorium. You were already ready that you're not going to come back to that barrack. But we were fortunate. We were really like, once a month, I think, or once in three weeks, we stripped and we showered, and we put back the same filthy clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, one day I wandered. And I'm going towards the kitchen. And there is a beautiful, gorgeous blue water swimming pool, back of the kitchen. And as I stand with the dress on, whatever, I don't think I took anything off. I jump in there. And I start swimming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And friend, for that minute, when I backstroke I thought the world was mine. And nobody saw me. Nobody shoot. And they could have shot me. They could have seen me. Nobody saw me that minute. I must have been there maybe 15-20 minutes. And absolutely nobody was in the pool. And this is July. This is hot weather and sweating. When I walked back to the barrack, my hair and my head is wet, and the dress I think was dripping. And by the time I walk, it's almost dry from the sun. My sister thought that I flipped my mind. She would not believe what I did. And I says, Edith, I was swimming. She looked at me [LAUGHING] as if I must have lost my mind that minute. I flipped. That's it. And I had to prove it to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she saw the layer of clothes under me was still a little bit, you know, wet. She believed me. She says, Eva, you could have been dead right there in the pool. All it took a gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow nobody saw me. And at that second, maybe 15 minutes of my life, I felt free. And I walked right back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, we were in Auschwitz still about September. By that time, I'm getting the typhus sort of working on me. I had diarrhea. The day they are transferring us, we don't know where we're going. But they're giving us warmer clothes. This is already cooling off in September.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're going to go, again, to the cattle car. And they shoved you in, the main concern to hold hands. We're in line, and it's almost us who's going to step out to the cattle car. And I have tremendous cramps. And I have to leave the line, because I'm not going to do it. And I have diarrhea, and run back. And my poor sister in line, and she doesn't know. And it was just maybe a-- a five minutes walk. But I couldn't do it. And I felt something snapped in me. I couldn't get up from that outhouse, what it was. It was this disgusting thing. But I still had to be there. Finally, I managed to get up and just make it before they get to our line. And we get up to the wagon. So as we went in there, well again, we're traveling about two days. We don't know where we're going. We arrive to a town, which we-- we don't see the town. It's like really a forest. And again, barracks, but not as Auschwitz. It's more normal. We had like maybe 30, 40 barracks, the whole thing, area. And we-- the women, and here we were assigned to work in a munition factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're together. And the food is a little bit better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Grunberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-R-U- dash on top, N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in Germany. That's in Germany. So in there, we're treated as workers. And like I said, in Auschwitz, they threw us a little bit of warmer clothes on us, and some kind of a coat. And in there we are assigned to the factory. And as the women, all the women, and in there when we get to the first day in the factory, you see men-- you see the Russians, political prisoners-- you see Polish people. And they look more nourished and more human than we looked. Because we, by that time, are getting done already. OK, everybody is getting skinnier, and everybody looks pale, and everybody looks sickly. And they look strong. They look healthy looking. And I was assigned to the assembly line where the bullets groove we were doing, and then a cap came on it. And my sister was putting the powder in. And she was in front of me. And we're still together, the five cousins. Nobody's sick, thank god. And this is already-- we're doing this from September, end of September-- no it's beginning of September. And the ration was OK, that it was holding us. But the cold weather started to come. And already in there in November, beginning of November, October we had snow. And our shoes was not a shoe. I remember having a wooden shoe with about two pieces of-- of rags around it that are as some kind of socks. And this is-- we marched to the factory, I would say about 3 kilometers. A few, long, long march. And it was a beautiful forest, and trees, you know, that beautiful German evergreens on two sides of the road. And we're marching. And we never see village people. We never see anybody except who worked in the munition factory, and who was in a camp. And in there, too, we had one rotten officer, who was-- we called her like a lesbian, mannish and mean. And oh, she enjoyed just somebody getting out of line, and hit them. But then we had a young girl who was 18 years old. And we think, girls, if she was watching us, because she had to be in a barrack with us, nighttime too. And when she would have a chance she would talk to some of the girls who fluently talked German, and telling us how she's 18 and she's going to have a baby. She doesn't even know who the husband is, and so forth and so on-- her life story. And she actually pitied us, and felt sorry. My 16th birthday in December 10, is in there in Grunberg. And my sister saves her ration of bread to give it to me next morning-- my birthday present. And I must have been the youngest in the whole group, I think. Because there was very few 14 and 15-year-olds left. And so they knew it. And I think my cousin, Magda, and all of them, they give me extra margarine or something. So I had a little packet under my pillow. And as I-- you know, when we get up, the first thing you have to make the bunk. Straighten the bunk before you get off. And you had a certain amount of time for that. And I'd make the bed and I see warm socks in there. And we knew, because she was that night in the barrack with us, the young girl, the German, who was actually nice and decent and kind. She never hit nobody-- nobody was ever by punished by her. And I looked at her, and I smiled. And I thank her. Danke, danke. And I knew it was her. And sure enough, I put the warm socks on. So I had to run, drag layer-- I had to throw off and it was better. And that was my 16th birthday there. So we were there until January.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after Grunberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Grunberg, they emptying again, we're hearing a bombing. And I have to tell you that factory was so camouflaged, so geniusly, that until you got to that factory door, you didn't see it's a building there. The evergreens, the greens, just like you see them in a movie, even better. It was unreal that how clever and how smart it was. And anyway, it comes New Year, end of New Year. And they said that I think the officer, the German soldier, the lady soldier tells somebody that yes, we are going to be leaving from there. They must leave because the enemy, of course, was Russia coming, or-- or whoever and the bombing you could hear already around there quite a bit already, getting closer and closer. So we are going to march. We don't know where we're going. We don't know the name of the camp. And January until the end of January, 30 days we're on the road to get to Bergen-Belsen. 30 days, snow all over, everywhere. Well, we're still together. Thank God nobody died from the five of us. And anybody who couldn't march, or couldn't walk, they just left behind. Sometimes they shot them, right there, shoot them right there in the ice, because they just couldn't walk. They give you always, I don't know how many kilometers, we would stop for a rest. And at certain times they would give us a ration, the bread and the margarine and some warm liquid or something. And then each night, we would stop at a farmer. But it always was so outskirts of a farm that you didn't see the public. You didn't see the people. You know how they went to the highways, and they managed to always get around those highways were nobody. At one point they had to take us through a little town. And I wish I would remember the name. Clean, shiny snow on the ground. We're walking by a butcher shop. And it's all glass-- just like in the shopping here, it would be a glass front. And we look in there, and it reflects-- the sun was shining, and it's winter, January. And we look in there, and we look at each other, and reflecting our body and the way we looked. It was unreal. We could not believe how we looked-- marching. And the salami is hanging in a window, and the kielbasas and all the meat hanging. And of course, at that point, we just cry like crazy. And never a soul you saw. Never a soul. But this was-- you know-- this must have been a small. Clean-cut village, but just beautifully clean. That was the only time we went through a small town, always outside, outskirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you arrive at Bergen-Belsen? And what was Bergen-Belsen like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bergen-Belsen, we arrived to a place, which reminded us of Auschwitz. There it was, the guard-- you know, the high tower of guards and barbed wires. But it wasn't as organized, unitly made, you know, the barracks. And all we see is a forest. It was the middle of a forest-- woods all over, and barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it didn't look like Auschwitz. Because Auschwitz had no woods-- I mean no trees or nothing. This was middle of the forest, middle of the woods, and barracks. And we're assigned to a barrack, and sure enough, who is not there already? Our cousin from Berezka, the two girls-- I showed you the picture of. And we are just sitting down-- a huge barrack assigned to 500 women, and as long as you-- you can-- I looked. There was just a long bare barrack and a floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are happened to sit like under one window. I don't know why we got there. But this is, all five of us. And then my two cousins is already there. So there we were seven cousins. So we we're in seven heaven. And in Bergen-Belsen already, what we experienced, they were not as cruel and not-- well, they counted us every morning. They started for the first two, three weeks. That's it. By that time, it's February. And it's freezing, and it's nobody outside-- they just count us. Instead they let us go in. In there, you could go in back to the barrack. So you're back in a barrack, and you're just sitting. And you're already weak. Most of them are just dying like flies. And started like in end of February, when my cousin started crying for her mother. [NON-ENGLISH], please just a little water. I just want to little water. Food was absolutely nothing already. Food was, after end of February, I think very little ration we got. I don't even know what we got, but very little. And by that time we did not go outside for a platz-- no more counting, no more nothing. We just sit like dead dogs. And everybody is, of course, weak, full of lice, your clothes is lice. You're laying on a wood floor. And that's when my sister-- my sister just ate, I think, what they give us. We all ate it. But that's it. And it was not even any physical to be outside, force yourself to walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because at that point, very few girls could get up and walk. OK? They were already sick. So we just laid and sit and talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the conversation was from morning until night, cooking. The girls cooking, you had to hear that. And especially-- not me, because I didn't cook, and I wouldn't know at that time yet how to make anything. And always remembering, reminiscing what would it be today.

speaker: interviewee
text: We kept-- we-- we wanted to keep our mind clear. So the only way they could do it, if they-- they concentrate at what day it is. Thursday, what would your parents do? What my mother's score was? What was it like every day, you know, the program?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when, of course Friday came, everybody with the chicken soup and how you make it, and cook. And this was the conversation from morning until night. And I thought about it. And I said to myself, I think it's a matter of mind stronger than-- than your stomach.

speaker: interviewee
text: That actually filled your mind up so that you didn't feel the hunger. You did not feel the hunger. They were talking about my sister, too, what's her favorite thing, and she loved blintzes, and how my mother made it, and what would be served. Now Friday, what would she do? So day by day, we talked and reminisced, what would it have been like when we were home. And then one day, which is already April, with the last week, last two weeks we don't get nothing-- nothing, no food. And everybody's dying everybody from hunger. So it started off, my cousin, Flora, whose sister survived and she's in Israel. And she cries, and she cries, finished. Next morning she is finished. But you had no-- by that time no Germany comes in to even look at you or count you. So actually we found that out after that we were already without the Germans in April. End of March, April, the last-- last two weeks of April, we had no German sight coming in. And we weren't strong enough to get up and really move around and see what the heck is going on outside. So just dying like flies from hunger. And a week later, the older sister, that one who I was working with [NON-ENGLISH], and she got the punishment, and I did-- Regina, her name, Regina Weiss-- she starts it. She started crying all night. And it was always nighttime, crying, [NON-ENGLISH], please, just a little water, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, she cried to her sister, Magda. Magda, bring- bring me a little water. Magda please. Next day and she-- And somehow you knew the sign. This is it. You always talked about the mother, and then by morning, they are dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they died, it was your business to get them out of the barrack. And we were in this opposite side of the barrack, near the window, and the door is over there across from me. And I probably-- my sister, my sister couldn't. She didn't get up at that time. My cousins and some other girls would help, and bring her as far as the hallway, out in front of the door-- hoping, or thinking that the German will pick them up and bury them, or whatever they're going to do. So when Magda dies, following week Regina died, we had only five of us, no, six of us-- and-- no, five-- and there is my sister. She's not moving. She's just sitting in this position, and just talking, keep on talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the English came in, it's April 15. All we know is a terrible noise and music and loud speakers comes on in Hungarian, in German, in English. Stay where you are. You are free. The war is over. And of course, when they get to the point, and we see the tank, and I see the first time in my life, you know, the kilt and the bagpipe playing. And we-- I-- I get up. I kneel up, actually. I kneel up towards then window, and I see them. And I want my sister to see. I say, Edith, please come. Get up, get up. And I'm helping her. I says, look, we are free. We made it. And she can't hardly get up. She says, I can't. And I couldn't either, anymore. I couldn't get up. But I got on my knees. And just then, and I show it to her. And we are just, you know, laughing, looking. And as you look, where you get up, and you look end of the barrack, it's empty. It's empty. We never paid attention how many already died. And was in front of the hallway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came in. And she says to me, Evika, you must go and see what's going on. And we know now we are free. So there's no German in sight. And I can't get up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she starts with me. She says, Evika, you must get up. Literally, with my knee, crawled through that door, hold onto the handle. And that's how I pulled myself up. And I walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I walk outside, and by-- after that-- by that time, my other cousin from the Weiss's, she was 18 or 19, gorgeous girl. She is screaming with horrible, horrible pain. It's hunger pain, probably, but you know what we thought? That she has appendix. Because she was just holding her stomach and screaming and all this. But then already the English are there. So when I get up, and Magda, I think, was-- she was able, and she was thank God, strong enough, stronger than me. She follows me. And there is my cousin on the ground in the hallway, what we put out there two weeks ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is my other cousin, and we literally just step over their dead bodies, all over in the hallway, and go outside. So I went out, and I don't know why. I just wandered around. And the gates, the iron gates, the wires with bars were all down on the ground. And men moving around, and women. So I crossed that area. And I went in for a while-- I think the first barrack I could see-- and went into the men barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they're the same. But they had bunk beds. And that surprised us, because we were just on the floor, no bunk bed, nothing. They were on a bunk, just like you see it in a movie. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's exactly how you saw it. It's just skeletons, frightening. You actually were afraid to look at them, because to you, they looked worse than we did, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wandered, and I looked at everything. Until I could walk-- and I looked at faces, maybe. Maybe I'll find my brother or somebody there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walked back. And I told Edith where I was. And by then, later, no next morning, now we-- we stayed in the barracks. Next morning they came, the English, introduced themselves. And they want to know who is the [NON-ENGLISH]. And sure enough, my cousin is still lasting, and she's, you know, with the pain, screaming. Right away they brought the stretcher, and they take her away. Then they take my sister away. And I'm screaming that I want to go. But she says, no, no, no. And there was always somebody translating-- no no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what's your name? And I tell the name. And I says, look, I'm with my sister until now. You're not going to separate us now. She says, no. She's going to the hospital. They will tell me where she is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the end. And they take her with the ambulance, and they take my cousin with the ambulance. And here I am, only Magda, me, and Ella, who's in this Israel, three of us left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ella was pretty strong, Magda was pretty strong. So Magda had to support me. And then they take us, and then of course we go into our-- oh, and then they come in first, the next morning, and who wants to eat? They give you whatever they had with them-- canned food. Can you imagine? Until today, when I open a can of baked beans, I think of Bergen-Belsen. That's where I tasted baked beans. Baked beans, you know, in a Heinz can, tuna fish.

speaker: interviewee
text: They open it up-- we didn't have no way of opening-- open it up, and if they're hungry, sure enough. I know I had baked beans. Well, that's all I needed. We had no bread, no food, nothing the last two weeks. There was nothing in our mouths. I think the one who was strong enough, they went outside and brought some snow in. That's what they did. And then we ate the snow. And then they come in. And they take us who could walk. They tell us, please get up. Who's walking, we're going to the building, which was Bergen-Belsen-- what it was called, I think Belsen. It was off, it was a town off from the camps, off from the barracks. And it's a huge, beautiful, like a sanatorium. I'll never forget. There was a beautiful park area, cul-de-sac, and magnificent buildings, two, three, four. They had to be a sanatorium for the Germans. But the English insisted to empty it. And they put us in there. So I had to go after they sanitized us, and sprayed us and strip us off, and give us clothes. Then we go into the building where regular beds, everything nice, bunk beds, everything clean, and where the person who was walking and strong enough to move around goes there. And of course, they fed us the best they could. I don't even remember. But we already had food. And my sister is in the hospital. Now that night, after we get into our building, I take off. And I am crying. Because I cannot find my sister. She's not in the same building with us, which of course, we realized.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I go from bed to bed all night. I don't know how I did it, to look for my sister. And the three building was nothing but hospital. I finally find her. And after I find her, you know, I'm staying in my building which was on this end.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a park area, across from that park, was her sanatorium, her hospital. They were spotless, snow white, the nurses with the German nurses, you know with a white cape. Just like you see in the old-fashioned movies, like when you see the you see those girls. And they always remind me of that. And the bed was clean. So thank God she is in clean bed. And she's very, very sick. How sick, I did not find out until the day before she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in June 30. So from April 15, until June, she was in a hospital. By that time, we get stronger. And, you know, we move around. And they say that, you know, who has TB or who's sick, they are assigned to go to Switzerland or Sweden. Now my idol as a kid, I always wanted to see Switzerland. And I'm disappointed that we are assigned to Sweden, OK, to go. And the transport is all made up, and we know that we're going to leave. I think it was July 1st or 2nd, to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you to lose your sister after going through--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the most horrible thing. And I didn't want to live. I was with her until her dying day. And I could see-- the day before, every time I took care of her and she's in bed, and I tried to slowly feed her, and they would bring like orange juice, some drinks. They brought already things like that-- and some crackers, you know, snacks. And I would do that feeding. I was always there, hanging around. No place. This was my life. And when I could, I wheeled her out to the terrace. And I was telling her, you know, showing her how gorgeous the park flowers, everything. And I says, we're going home, Edith. We're going home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally the last week she didn't let me wheel her out in a wheelchair, nothing. So when I sponged her, she never let me sponge below this, below her waist, never. Just the back and front, a little, and face. You know, and all that, and we talked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't even know what we were talking about. Finally one day, when she's very weak, and I says Edith, we're going to Sweden. I really don't know what Sweden is like. But we're going in four days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says, Evika, I'm not going anywhere. I says, what do you mean, you're not going? You are going. They got sanatoriums. You are transferred there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she pulls off the sheet, and shows me her whole side-- friend, eaten up, just eaten up-- they call that in Europe, [NON-ENGLISH]. Her body was eaten up from the lice and the raw soreness. And she was very weak. But in between this time, she had an English colonel, the doctor. And I would go there when the colonel was there. And my sister wrote a letter to America, to my cousin the doctor in Washington, to please send us penicillin. And she knew the address. And we corresponded with the American relatives until '40. And she knew the cousins, because one cousin was back, and honeymooning in our town in 1938 when she brought her husband out, the doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we send a letter. And we're hoping that really medicine will arrive, and my sister will be better. So that day, she shows me herself. And I just cry. And I said, then, I says, Edith, but you're going to be all right. No Evika, I'm not going anywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so when I'm staying there, with her that night, she was cold. She couldn't get air. She says, she's just choking. You know, it was her last breath. And I'm sitting at the window, and I'm with her. And I don't know what I'm thinking of. By that point, they didn't chase me out of the room. Because nighttime they would chase me. Then I go back. And--
